content.text:
# Blind Hunter Findings — commit 84affc3

## HIGH

- **Stub commands are still reachable after `exit 1` via `;` separator** — HIGH
  Evidence: `"release": "echo '[DEPRECATED] ...' && exit 1 ; npm run release:patch"` (and same pattern on every `release:*` script).
  Rationale: In POSIX sh, `;` is a sequencing operator that runs the next command regardless of the previous exit status. `echo` virtually always succeeds, so `&& exit 1` terminates the shell — but if anything ever makes `echo` fail (closed stdout pipe, etc.) or a user edits the alias, the stanza silently falls through to the real release command. The "fail-loud stub" invariant rests on `echo` never failing; the `;` branch should have been `&&` with a guaranteed-false command, or simply omit the trailing command entirely.

- **Double-print and double-exit UX on `npm run release`** — HIGH
  Evidence: `"release": "echo '[DEPRECATED] ...' && exit 1 ; npm run release:patch"` chains to `release:patch`, which itself prints the same deprecation and exits 1.
  Rationale: The outer `exit 1` means the inner `npm run release:patch` is never reached under normal conditions, so the "chain to release:patch" is dead code — but the CONTRIBUTING.md claim that "the bare `release` alias" is a fail-loud stub is half-true: it fails loud once, not twice. The dead tail is misleading to any maintainer reading the scripts. Drop it.

## MEDIUM

- **Cross-shell portability: `;` and `&&` behave differently (or not at all) under cmd.exe** — MEDIUM
  Evidence: `"release": "echo '[DEPRECATED] ...' && exit 1 ; npm run release:patch"`.
  Rationale: npm on Windows runs scripts through `cmd.exe` by default unless `script-shell` is set. `cmd.exe` does not treat `;` as a command separator the way POSIX sh does — it passes the remainder as literal arguments to the preceding command in some contexts, and `exit 1` in cmd exits the shell immediately (no chained command). The single-quoted `echo` string is also parsed differently under cmd (single quotes are not special). Behaviour is shell-dependent and not guaranteed to print a clean deprecation line on Windows.

- **Deprecation message goes to stdout, not stderr** — MEDIUM
  Evidence: `"echo '[DEPRECATED] Use .github/workflows/release.yaml via GitHub Actions. See docs/RELEASING.md.'"`.
  Rationale: Deprecation/error notices conventionally go to stderr so they're visible when callers pipe stdout. `echo ... >&2` would be more correct — especially given the script exits non-zero, suggesting the message is diagnostic.

- **CONTRIBUTING claims verbatim text that does not match package.json** — MEDIUM
  Evidence: Docs say the stub prints `[DEPRECATED] Use .github/workflows/release.yaml via GitHub Actions.` (period, full stop). Actual script prints `[DEPRECATED] Use .github/workflows/release.yaml via GitHub Actions. See docs/RELEASING.md.`.
  Rationale: The bulleted prose truncates the real message at the first period. Readers grepping their terminal output for the exact quoted string won't find a match. Either widen the quote or add an ellipsis.

- **`version_bump` choices include `alpha`/`beta`/`rc` which are not standard `npm version` prerelease identifiers** — MEDIUM (suspicion)
  Evidence: Doc lists `version_bump` (`alpha` / `beta` / `rc` / `patch` / `minor` / `major`).
  Rationale: `npm version` accepts `prerelease`, `prepatch`, `preminor`, `premajor`, `patch`, `minor`, `major` — not bare `alpha`/`beta`/`rc`. Those are tag labels (`--preid`), not bump kinds. Without seeing the workflow, this smells like the doc is listing UI choice labels; if the workflow actually maps `alpha` → `npm version prerelease --preid=alpha`, fine — but a reader would reasonably expect the list to match a known CLI vocabulary.

- **Epic 6 / `_bmad-output` style references leak implementation jargon into public docs** — MEDIUM

content.text:
# Blind Hunter Findings — commit 84affc3

## HIGH

- **Stub commands are still reachable after `exit 1` via `;` separator** — HIGH
  Evidence: `"release": "echo '[DEPRECATED] ...' && exit 1 ; npm run release:patch"` (and same pattern on every `release:*` script).
  Rationale: In POSIX sh, `;` is a sequencing operator that runs the next command regardless of the previous exit status. `echo` virtually always succeeds, so `&& exit 1` terminates the shell — but if anything ever makes `echo` fail (closed stdout pipe, etc.) or a user edits the alias, the stanza silently falls through to the real release command. The "fail-loud stub" invariant rests on `echo` never failing; the `;` branch should have been `&&` with a guaranteed-false command, or simply omit the trailing command entirely.

- **Double-print and double-exit UX on `npm run release`** — HIGH
  Evidence: `"release": "echo '[DEPRECATED] ...' && exit 1 ; npm run release:patch"` chains to `release:patch`, which itself prints the same deprecation and exits 1.
  Rationale: The outer `exit 1` means the inner `npm run release:patch` is never reached under normal conditions, so the "chain to release:patch" is dead code — but the CONTRIBUTING.md claim that "the bare `release` alias" is a fail-loud stub is half-true: it fails loud once, not twice. The dead tail is misleading to any maintainer reading the scripts. Drop it.

## MEDIUM

- **Cross-shell portability: `;` and `&&` behave differently (or not at all) under cmd.exe** — MEDIUM
  Evidence: `"release": "echo '[DEPRECATED] ...' && exit 1 ; npm run release:patch"`.
  Rationale: npm on Windows runs scripts through `cmd.exe` by default unless `script-shell` is set. `cmd.exe` does not treat `;` as a command separator the way POSIX sh does — it passes the remainder as literal arguments to the preceding command in some contexts, and `exit 1` in cmd exits the shell immediately (no chained command). The single-quoted `echo` string is also parsed differently under cmd (single quotes are not special). Behaviour is shell-dependent and not guaranteed to print a clean deprecation line on Windows.

- **Deprecation message goes to stdout, not stderr** — MEDIUM
  Evidence: `"echo '[DEPRECATED] Use .github/workflows/release.yaml via GitHub Actions. See docs/RELEASING.md.'"`.
  Rationale: Deprecation/error notices conventionally go to stderr so they're visible when callers pipe stdout. `echo ... >&2` would be more correct — especially given the script exits non-zero, suggesting the message is diagnostic.

- **CONTRIBUTING claims verbatim text that does not match package.json** — MEDIUM
  Evidence: Docs say the stub prints `[DEPRECATED] Use .github/workflows/release.yaml via GitHub Actions.` (period, full stop). Actual script prints `[DEPRECATED] Use .github/workflows/release.yaml via GitHub Actions. See docs/RELEASING.md.`.
  Rationale: The bulleted prose truncates the real message at the first period. Readers grepping their terminal output for the exact quoted string won't find a match. Either widen the quote or add an ellipsis.

- **`version_bump` choices include `alpha`/`beta`/`rc` which are not standard `npm version` prerelease identifiers** — MEDIUM (suspicion)
  Evidence: Doc lists `version_bump` (`alpha` / `beta` / `rc` / `patch` / `minor` / `major`).
  Rationale: `npm version` accepts `prerelease`, `prepatch`, `preminor`, `premajor`, `patch`, `minor`, `major` — not bare `alpha`/`beta`/`rc`. Those are tag labels (`--preid`), not bump kinds. Without seeing the workflow, this smells like the doc is listing UI choice labels; if the workflow actually maps `alpha` → `npm version prerelease --preid=alpha`, fine — but a reader would reasonably expect the list to match a known CLI vocabulary.

- **Epic 6 / `_bmad-output` style references leak implementation jargon into public docs** — MEDIUM
  Evidence: "Epic 6 removes them outright post-v1.0.0" and "scheduled for deletion post-v1.0.0 per Epic 6".
  Rationale: External contributors (CONTRIBUTING.md is outward-facing) have no way to resolve "Epic 6." Link to a tracking issue or drop the epic reference.

## LOW

- **"Seven-scenario rollback playbook" vs. recent commit "six-scenario rollback playbook"** — LOW (suspicion)
  Evidence: Doc line: "the seven-scenario [rollback playbook](docs/RELEASING.md#rollback-playbook)". Git log (shown in your intro, not the diff) shows commit `4d0c618 docs(releasing): author six-scenario rollback playbook`.
  Rationale: Either the playbook grew by one scenario (plausible — `e597825` applied 12 patches) or the count is stale. From the diff alone this is not confirmable, but the mismatch is a yellow flag worth verifying against `docs/RELEASING.md`.

- **Anchor `#rollback-playbook` is not guaranteed to exist** — LOW (suspicion)
  Evidence: `[rollback playbook](docs/RELEASING.md#rollback-playbook)`.
  Rationale: GitHub auto-generates heading anchors from heading text; a heading like "Rollback Playbook" yields `#rollback-playbook` (correct), but "The Rollback Playbook" or "Rollback playbook (six scenarios)" would not. Unverifiable from this diff.

- **Em-dash density in a short section** — LOW / NIT
  Evidence: Five em-dashes in seven lines ("Maintainers only —", "only supported route —", "retained only as a fail-loud stub ...", "full procedure —", "its required-reviewer gate, npm Trusted Publisher registration, and the seven-scenario ...").
  Rationale: Screen readers read em-dashes as pauses or spell them out depending on settings; heavy use hurts prosody and scanability. Consider replacing some with periods or parenthetical commas.

- **Tone: "Maintainers only — if you're not cutting a release, skip this section."** — NIT
  Evidence: as quoted.
  Rationale: Reads fine to me — informative, not condescending. You flagged this as a focus area; I don't see a problem. It's a standard gating sentence.

## NIT

- **Double negative phrasing "muscle memory doesn't accidentally ship anything"** — NIT
  Evidence: "The stubs exist so that muscle memory doesn't accidentally ship anything".
  Rationale: Clear enough, but "so that muscle-memory `npm run release` invocations can't ship anything" reads tighter.

- **Bare CLI exit code is `1`, which conflates "deprecated" with generic failure** — NIT
  Evidence: `&& exit 1`.
  Rationale: Some projects use `exit 2` or a documented sentinel code for deprecation to distinguish from real failure in CI logs. Minor.

---

**Nothing worth flagging** at tiers above HIGH. No security issues spotted in this diff (no shell injection surface — the strings are static literals). No JSON syntax issues in package.json.

content.text:
Critical finding: the `[DEPRECATED]` message is on **stdout**, not stderr. A CI log scraper that greps stderr for errors would miss it. More importantly, a user piping `npm run release:patch | grep ...` might mask the warning. This is LOW severity because the user is intentionally running this command.

Another concern — the message doesn't include a timestamp/indication it's from npm's script. If someone's console has multiple concurrent outputs, the message might be lost. NIT at best.

One more: `release:prerelease` — the workflow has `alpha/beta/rc` choices but the stub forwards (blocked) to `npm version prerelease`. The CONTRIBUTING.md correctly lists `alpha / beta / rc / patch / minor / major` as workflow choices, which matches `release.yaml`. No drift found.

Let me also verify one last thing — what if someone runs `npm run release:patch --force` or similar? Let me read how npm handles passthrough args:

npm 10+ passes extra args after `--` to the script. Without `--`, npm 10 treats certain flags as its own config. Either way, the echo+exit 1 fires first so it's moot.

Let me look at whether CONTRIBUTING.md's claim of "seven-scenario" matches the actual 7 scenarios (A-G):

Already confirmed above: 7 scenarios A through G. Matches.

One last check — is `CONTRIBUTING.md` listed in lint-staged config such that prettier might reformat it on stage? Looking at line 77 config... `*.md` → `markdownlint-cli2`. Prettier doesn't rewrite *.md, markdownlint doesn't auto-format. Good.

Now let me also check: does the `echo '...'` with single quotes hold up if `scripts` are processed by prettier-plugin-packagejson? The single quotes are inside a JSON string, so they're literal. Let me also check if prettier would reformat them — already tested `prettier --check` passes.

Let me verify one final concern — if a user's shell is non-POSIX (unusual `$SHELL`), npm uses `/bin/sh` by default regardless, unless `script-shell` is set. Tested dash works (dash is the default sh on Debian/Ubuntu). OK.

I now have enough evidence. Let me produce the findings.

---

# Edge Case Hunt — Commit `84affc3` (Story 4.2)

## Enumeration coverage

| Invocation surface | Status |
|---|---|
| `npm run release` (bare alias) | Tested — exits 1 on stdout |
| `npm run release:{major,minor,patch,prerelease}` | Tested — all exit 1 on stdout |
| `npm run release -- --arg` (forwarded args) | Verified — args appended after `;` but preempted by `exit 1` |
| `npm run release:patch --silent` / `--loglevel=silent` | Verified — `[DEPRECATED]` text still reaches the user |
| Direct `sh -c`, `bash -c`, `dash -c` on the stanza | All three exit 1 correctly; `;` cleanly terminates |
| Windows / cmd.exe | Not a concern — `CONTRIBUTING.md:21` declares Windows unsupported |
| Markdown anchor `#rollback-playbook` on feat branch | Exists at `docs/RELEASING.md:183` |
| Markdown anchor `#rollback-playbook` on `main` | Exists as a one-line **placeholder stub** predating Story 4.1 |
| `docs/RELEASING.md` file presence on main | Present (185 lines, stub form) |
| `release.yaml` `version_bump` choices vs CONTRIBUTING | Match exactly: `alpha / beta / rc / patch / minor / major` |
| Any CI step running `npm run release:*` | None found (grep of `.github/workflows/*`, `package.json` scripts) |
| `release.yaml` internal invocation of `npm run release:*` | None — uses `npm version` directly (safe) |
| `prettier-plugin-packagejson` reorder risk | `prettier --check` passes; order preserved |
| `validate-doc-links`/`validate-file-refs` coverage of CONTRIBUTING.md | Neither validator scans CONTRIBUTING.md |
| `markdownlint-cli2` on new section | Clean (0 errors) |

## Findings

### 1. Anchor-drift in CONTRIBUTING.md → docs/RELEASING.md is not CI-guarded — severity: LOW

- **Edge case.** `CONTRIBUTING.md:87` hardcodes `[rollback playbook](docs/RELEASING.md#rollback-playbook)`. If a future RELEASING.md refactor renames the heading (e.g., `## Rollback Recipes`, or adds a prefix like `## Release / Rollback Playbook`), the CONTRIBUTING.md link silently 404s on GitHub with no CI signal.
- **Evidence.** `tools/validate-doc-links.js` scans only `docs/` (verified by running — "Validating docs in: .../docs"). `tools/validate-file-refs.js` scans only `src/`. `grep -r "rollback-playbook" tools/` returns zero matches.
- **Why it matters.** Story 4.2 introduces a cross-file anchor dependency that bypasses the validator net. The rest of the repo's doc-link hygiene is automated; this one is not.

### 2. Intermediate-state readers of `main` see broken-looking rollback link — severity: LOW

- **Edge case.** If Story 4.2 (`84affc3`) lands on `main` before Story 4.1's 552-line RELEASING.md does (or during a partial-merge window), readers of CONTRIBUTING.md on main follow `docs/RELEASING.md#rollback-playbook` and hit the pre-Story-4.1 placeholder stub (`"To be authored in Story 4.1 — rollback playbook covering scenarios such as ..."`). The anchor resolves (GitHub finds `## Rollback Playbook` at line 183 on main), but the content is one sentence, not the promised "seven-scenario playbook" CONTRIBUTING.md advertises.
- **Evidence.** `git show main:docs/RELEASING.md | tail -3` shows the placeholder; `git log main..feat/release-workflow` shows Stories 4.1 + 4.2 both ahead of main and not yet merged.
- **Why it matters.** This only bites if the feature branch is merged piecemeal or if someone cherry-picks just 4.2. The happy path (whole-branch merge) is fine. Flag for the merge sequence.

### 3. `[DEPRECATED]` message lands on stdout, not stderr — severity: LOW

- **Edge case.** `echo '[DEPRECATED] ...' && exit 1` writes to **stdout**. A contributor piping output (`npm run release:patch | tee build.log`, or CI log-scraper patterns that discriminate stdout-output vs stderr-warnings) sees the warning mingled with normal output. Verified: `npm run release:patch 1>/dev/null` prints nothing; `2>/dev/null` still prints the message.
- **Evidence.** `package.json:48-52` use `echo '...'` (no `>&2` redirect).
- **Why it matters.** Minor UX — the message still reaches an interactive terminal. But for automation, a deprecation warning on stderr is the conventional home. Consider `echo '[DEPRECATED] ...' >&2 && exit 1 ;` if this ever matters.

### 4. Trailing `; npm version ...` tail is dead code but still an attack/confusion surface — severity: NIT

- **Edge case.** The preserved tail (`; npm version patch && git push --follow-tags`) is unreachable under any POSIX shell because `exit 1` terminates the `sh -c` subshell before the `;` separator is reached. Critical finding: the `[DEPRECATED]` message is on **stdout**, not stderr. A CI log scraper that greps stderr for errors would miss it. More importantly, a user piping `npm run release:patch | grep ...` might mask the warning. This is LOW severity because the user is intentionally running this command.

One more: `release:prerelease` — the workflow has `alpha/beta/rc` choices but the stub forwards (blocked) to `npm version prerelease`. The CONTRIBUTING.md correctly lists `alpha / beta / rc / patch / minor / major` as workflow choices, which matches `release.yaml`. No drift found.

Let me also verify one last thing — what if someone runs `npm run release:patch --force` or similar? Let me read how npm handles passthrough args:

npm 10+ passes extra args after `--` to the script. Without `--`, npm 10 treats certain flags as its own config. Either way, the echo+exit 1 fires first so it's moot.

Let me look at whether CONTRIBUTING.md's claim of "seven-scenario" matches the actual 7 scenarios (A-G):

Already confirmed above: 7 scenarios A through G. Matches.

One last check — is `CONTRIBUTING.md` listed in lint-staged config such that prettier might reformat it on stage? Looking at line 77 config... `*.md` → `markdownlint-cli2`. Prettier doesn't rewrite *.md, markdownlint doesn't auto-format. Good.

Now let me also check: does the `echo '...'` with single quotes hold up if `scripts` are processed by prettier-plugin-packagejson? The single quotes are inside a JSON string, so they're literal. Let me also check if prettier would reformat them — already tested `prettier --check` passes.

Let me verify one final concern — if a user's shell is non-POSIX (unusual `$SHELL`), npm uses `/bin/sh` by default regardless, unless `script-shell` is set. Tested dash works (dash is the default sh on Debian/Ubuntu). OK.

# Step 3: Triage

## RULES

- YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT in your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}`
- Be precise. When uncertain between categories, prefer the more conservative classification.

## INSTRUCTIONS

1. **Normalize** findings into a common format. Expected input formats:
   - Adversarial (Blind Hunter): markdown list of descriptions
   - Edge Case Hunter: JSON array with `location`, `trigger_condition`, `guard_snippet`, `potential_consequence` fields
   - Acceptance Auditor: markdown list with title, AC/constraint reference, and evidence

   If a layer's output does not match its expected format, attempt best-effort parsing. Note any parsing issues for the user.

   Convert all to a unified list where each finding has:
   - `id` -- sequential integer
   - `source` -- `blind`, `edge`, `auditor`, or merged sources (e.g., `blind+edge`)
   - `title` -- one-line summary
   - `detail` -- full description
   - `location` -- file and line reference (if available)

2. **Deduplicate.** If two or more findings describe the same issue, merge them into one:
   - Use the most specific finding as the base (prefer edge-case JSON with location over adversarial prose).
   - Append any unique detail, reasoning, or location references from the other finding(s) into the surviving `detail` field.
   - Set `source` to the merged sources (e.g., `blind+edge`).

3. **Classify** each finding into exactly one bucket:
   - **decision_needed** -- There is an ambiguous choice that requires human input. The code cannot be correctly patched without knowing the user's intent. Only possible if `{review_mode}` = `"full"`.
   - **patch** -- Code issue that is fixable without human input. The correct fix is unambiguous.
   - **defer** -- Pre-existing issue not caused by the current change. Real but not actionable now.
   - **dismiss** -- Noise, false positive, or handled elsewhere.

   If `{review_mode}` = `"no-spec"` and a finding would otherwise be `decision_needed`, reclassify it as `patch` (if the fix is unambiguous) or `defer` (if not).

4. **Drop** all `dismiss` findings. Record the dismiss count for the summary.

5. If `{failed_layers}` is non-empty, report which layers failed before announcing results. If zero findings remain after dropping dismissed AND `{failed_layers}` is non-empty, warn the user that the review may be incomplete rather than announcing a clean review.

6. If zero findings remain after triage (all rejected or none raised): state "✅ Clean review — all layers passed." (Step 3 already warned if any review layers failed via `{failed_layers}`.)


## NEXT

Read fully and follow `./step-04-present.md`

Clean review shortcut 16 17 If zero findings remain after triage (all dismissed or none raised): state that and proceed to section 6 (Sprint Status Update). 18 19 ### 2. Write findings to the story file 20 21 If `{spec_file}` exists and contains a Tasks/Subtasks section, append a `### Review Findings` subsection. Write all findings in this order: 22 23 1. **`decision-needed`** findings (unchecked): 24 `- [ ] [Review][Decision] — <Detail>` 25 26 2. **`patch`** findings (unchecked): 27 `- [ ] [Review][Patch] <Title> [<file>:<line>]` 28 29 3. **`defer`** findings (checked off, marked deferred): 30 `- [x] [Review][Defer] <Title> [<file>:<line>] — deferred, pre-existing` 31 32 Also append each `defer` finding to `{deferred_work_file}` under a heading `## Deferred from: code review ({date})`. If `{spec_file}` is set, include its basename in the heading (e.g., `code review of story-3.3 (2026-03-18)`). One bullet per finding with description. 33 34 ### 3. Present summary 35 36 Announce what was written: 37 38 > **Code review complete.** <D> `decision-needed`, <P> `patch`, <W> `defer`, <R> dismissed as noise. 39 40 If `{spec_file}` is set, add: `Findings written to the review findings section in {spec_file}.` 41 Otherwise add: `Findings are listed above. No story file was provided, so nothing was persisted.` 42 43 ### 4. Resolve decision-needed findings 44 45 If `decision_needed` findings exist, present each one with its detail and the options available. The user must decide — the correct fix is ambiguous without their input. Walk through each finding (or batch related ones) and get the user's call. Once resolved, each becomes a `patch`, `defer`, or is dismissed. 46 47 If the user chooses to defer, ask: Quick one-line reason for deferring this item? (helps future reviews): — then append that reason to both the story file bullet and the `{deferred_work_file}` entry. 48 49 **HALT** — I am waiting for your numbered choice. Reply with only the number (or "0" for batch). Do not proceed until you select an option. 50 51 ### 5. Handle `patch` findings 52 53 If `patch` findings exist (including any resolved from step 4), HALT. Ask the user: 54 55 If `{spec_file}` is set, present all three options (if >3 `patch` findings exist, also show option 0): 56 57 > **How would you like to handle the <Z> `patch` findings?** 58 > 0. **Batch-apply all** — automatically fix every non-controversial patch (recommended when there are many) 59 > 1. **Fix them automatically** — I will apply fixes now 60 > 2. **Leave as action items** — they are already in the story file 61 > 3. **Walk through each** — let me show details before deciding 62 63 If `{spec_file}` is **not** set, present only options 1 and 3 (omit option 2 — findings were not written to a file). If >3 `patch` findings exist, also show option 0: 64 65 > **How would you like to handle the <Z> `patch` findings?** 66 > 0. **Batch-apply all** — automatically fix every non-controversial patch (recommended when there are many) 67 > 1. **Fix them automatically** — I will apply fixes now 68 > 2. **Walk through each** — let me show details before deciding 69 70 **HALT** — I am waiting for your numbered choice. Reply with only the number (or "0" for batch). Do not proceed until you select an option. 71 72 - **Option 0** (only when >3 findings): Apply all non-controversial patches without per-finding confirmation. Skip any finding that requires judgment. Present a summary of changes made and any skipped findings. 73 - **Option 1**: Apply each fix. After all patches are applied, present a summary of changes made. If `{spec_file}` is set, check off the items in the story file. 74 - **Option 2** (only when `{spec_file}` is set): Done — findings are already written to the story. 75 - **Walk through each**: Present each finding with full detail, diff context, and suggested fix. After walkthrough, re-offer the applicable options above. 76 77 **HALT** — I am waiting for your numbered choice. Reply with only the number (or "0" for batch). Do not proceed until you select an option. 78 79 **✅ Code review actions complete** 80 81 - Decision-needed resolved: <D> 82 - Patches handled: <P> 83 - Deferred: <W> 84 - Dismissed: <R> 85 86 ### 6. Update story status and sync sprint tracking 87 88 Skip this section if `{spec_file}` is not set. 89 90 #### Determine new status based on review outcome 91 92 - If all `decision-needed` and `patch` findings were resolved (fixed or dismissed) AND no unresolved HIGH/MEDIUM issues remain: set `{new_status}` = `done`. Update the story file Status section to `done`. 93 - If `patch` findings were left as action items, or unresolved issues remain: set `{new_status}` = `in-progress`. Update the story file Status section to `in-progress`. 94 95 Save the story file. 96 97 #### Sync sprint-status.yaml 98 99 If `{story_key}` is not set, skip this subsection and note that sprint status was not synced because no story key was available. 100 101 If `{sprint_status}` file exists: 102 103 1. Load the FULL `{sprint_status}` file. 104 2. Find the `development_status` entry matching `{story_key}`. 105 3. If found: update `development_status[{story_key}]` to `{new_status}`. Update `last_updated` to current date. Save the file, preserving ALL comments and structure including STATUS DEFINITIONS. 106 4. If `{story_key}` not found in sprint status: warn the user that the story file was updated but sprint-status sync failed. 107 108 If `{sprint_status}` file does not exist, note that story status was updated in the story file only. 109 110 #### Completion summary 111 112 > **Review Complete!** 113 > 114 > **Story Status:** `{new_status}` 115 > **Issues Fixed:** <fixed_count> 116 > **Action Items Created:** <action_count> 117 > **Deferred:** <W> 118 > **Dismissed:** <R> 119 120 ### 7. Next steps 121 122 Present the user with follow-up options: 123 124 > **What would you like to do next?** 125 > 1. **Start the next story** — run `dev-story` to pick up the next `ready-for-dev` story 126 > 2. **Re-run code review** — address findings and review again 127 > 3. **Done** — end the workflow 128 129 **HALT** — I am waiting for your choice. Do not proceed until the user selects an option. 130

file.content: --- deferred_work_file: '{implementation_artifacts}/deferred-work.md' --- # Step 4: Present and Act ## RULES - YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT in your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}` - When `{spec_file}` is set, always write findings to the story file before offering action choices. - `decision-needed` findings must be resolved before handling `patch` findings. ## INSTRUCTIONS ### 1. Clean review shortcut If zero findings remain after triage (all dismissed or none raised): state that and proceed to section 6 (Sprint Status Update). ### 2. Write findings to the story file If `{spec_file}` exists and contains a Tasks/Subtasks section, append a `### Review Findings` subsection. Write all findings in this order: 1. **`decision-needed`** findings (unchecked): `- [ ] [Review][Decision] — <Detail>` 2. **`patch`** findings (unchecked): `- [ ] [Review][Patch] <Title> [<file>:<line>]` 3. **`defer`** findings (checked off, marked deferred): `- [x] [Review][Defer] <Title> [<file>:<line>] — deferred, pre-existing` Also append each `defer` finding to `{deferred_work_file}` under a heading `## Deferred from: code review ({date})`. If `{spec_file}` is set, include its basename in the heading (e.g., `code review of story-3.3 (2026-03-18)`). One bullet per finding with description. ### 3. Present summary Announce what was written: > **Code review complete.** <D> `decision-needed`, <P> `patch`, <W> `defer`, <R> dismissed as noise. If `{spec_file}` is set, add: `Findings written to the review findings section in {spec_file}.` Otherwise add: `Findings are listed above. No story file was provided, so nothing was persisted.` ### 4. Resolve decision-needed findings If `decision_needed` findings exist, present each one with its detail and the options available. The user must decide — the correct fix is ambiguous without their input. Walk through each finding (or batch related ones) and get the user's call. Once resolved, each becomes a `patch`, `defer`, or is dismissed. If the user chooses to defer, ask: Quick one-line reason for deferring this item? (helps future reviews): — then append that reason to both the story file bullet and the `{deferred_work_file}` entry. **HALT** — I am waiting for your numbered choice. Reply with only the number (or "0" for batch). Do not proceed until you select an option. ### 5. Handle `patch` findings If `patch` findings exist (including any resolved from step 4), HALT. Ask the user: If `{spec_file}` is set, present all three options (if >3 `patch` findings exist, also show option 0): > **How would you like to handle the <Z> `patch` findings?** > 0. **Batch-apply all** — automatically fix every non-controversial patch (recommended when there are many) > 1. **Fix them automatically** — I will apply fixes now > 2. **Leave as action items** — they are already in the story file > 3. **Walk through each** — let me show details before deciding If `{spec_file}` is **not** set, present only options 1 and 3 (omit option 2 — findings were not written to a file). If >3 `patch` findings exist, also show option 0: > **How would you like to handle the <Z> `patch` findings?** > 0. **Batch-apply all** — automatically fix every non-controversial patch (recommended when there are many) > 1. **Fix them automatically** — I will apply fixes now > 2. **Walk through each** — let me show details before deciding **HALT** — I am waiting for your numbered choice. Reply with only the number (or "0" for batch). Do not proceed until you select an option. - **Option 0** (only when >3 findings): Apply all non-controversial patches without per-finding confirmation. Skip any finding that requires judgment. Present a summary of changes made and any skipped findings. - **Option 1**: Apply each fix. After all patches are applied, present a summary of changes made. If `{spec_file}` is set, check off the items in the story file. - **Option 2** (only when `{spec_file}` is set): Done — findings are already written to the story. - **Walk through each**: Present each finding with full detail, diff context, and suggested fix. After walkthrough, re-offer the applicable options above. **HALT** — I am waiting for your numbered choice. Reply with only the number (or "0" for batch). Do not proceed until you select an option. **✅ Code review actions complete** - Decision-needed resolved: <D> - Patches handled: <P> - Deferred: <W> - Dismissed: <R> ### 6. Update story status and sync sprint tracking Skip this section if `{spec_file}` is not set. #### Determine new status based on review outcome - If all `decision-needed` and `patch` findings were resolved (fixed or dismissed) AND no unresolved HIGH/MEDIUM issues remain: set `{new_status}` = `done`. Update the story file Status section to `done`. - If `patch` findings were left as action items, or unresolved issues remain: set `{new_status}` = `in-progress`. Update the story file Status section to `in-progress`. Save the story file. #### Sync sprint-status.yaml If `{story_key}` is not set, skip this subsection and note that sprint status was not synced because no story key was available. If `{sprint_status}` file exists: 1. Load the FULL `{sprint_status}` file. 2. Find the `development_status` entry matching `{story_key}`. 3. If found: update `development_status[{story_key}]` to `{new_status}`. Update `last_updated` to current date. Save the file, preserving ALL comments and structure including STATUS DEFINITIONS. 4. If `{story_key}` not found in sprint status: warn the user that the story file was updated but sprint-status sync failed. If `{sprint_status}` file does not exist, note that story status was updated in the story file only. #### Completion summary > **Review Complete!** > > **Story Status:** `{new_status}` > **Issues Fixed:** <fixed_count> > **Action Items Created:** <action_count> > **Deferred:** <W> > **Dismissed:** <R> ### 7. Next steps Present the user with follow-up options: > **What would you like to do next?** > 1. **Start the next story** — run `dev-story` to pick up the next `ready-for-dev` story > 2. **Re-run code review** — address findings and review again > 3. **Done** — end the workflow **HALT** — I am waiting for your choice. ### Change Log

| Date       | Story | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                     | Author |
| ---------- | ----- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------ |
| 2026-04-23 | 4.2   | Added `## Releasing` section to CONTRIBUTING.md naming the canonical `release.yaml` path; prefixed all five `release*` scripts in `package.json` with a fail-loud `[DEPRECATED]` stanza. Commit `84affc3`. | Amelia |

input.old_string: | 2026-04-23 | 4.2   | Added `## Releasing` section to CONTRIBUTING.md naming the canonical `release.yaml` path; prefixed all five `release*` scripts in `package.json` with a fail-loud `[DEPRECATED]` stanza. Commit `84affc3`. | Amelia |
input.new_string:
| 2026-04-23 | 4.2   | Added `## Releasing` section to CONTRIBUTING.md naming the canonical `release.yaml` path; prefixed all five `release*` scripts in `package.json` with a fail-loud `[DEPRECATED]` stanza. Commit `84affc3`. | Amelia |

### Review Findings

_Code review against commit `84affc3` on 2026-04-23 — 3 layers: Blind Hunter (no context), Edge Case Hunter (repo read), Acceptance Auditor (spec + 15 ACs). 15 findings raised, 11 dismissed. Acceptance Auditor verdict: all 15 ACs PASS (AC 6/11/12 are runtime-only and trust the Dev Agent Record smoke-test transcript)._

- [ ] [Review][Decision] Deprecation message on stdout instead of stderr — `package.json:48-52` writes `echo '[DEPRECATED] ...' && exit 1 ; tail`. Convention is that deprecation/diagnostic warnings go to stderr (`echo '...' >&2`). Current behavior: the message still reaches an interactive terminal but a user piping `npm run release:patch | tee build.log` or similar will see the warning mingled with normal stdout output, and automation that separates stdout from stderr won't classify it as a warning. Decision: AC 5 fixes the prefix string verbatim — adding `>&2` diverges from the spec literal. Accept spec as-is (dismiss) or diverge for convention (patch)?
- [ ] [Review][Decision] Internal "Epic 6" jargon leaks into outward-facing CONTRIBUTING.md — `CONTRIBUTING.md:84-85` says "Epic 6 removes them outright post-v1.0.0" and "scheduled for deletion post-v1.0.0 per Epic 6". External contributors reading CONTRIBUTING.md have no way to resolve "Epic 6" — it's a `_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/epics.md` reference invisible from outside the repo's internal planning tree. Decision: keep the Epic reference (internal-consistency signal — everyone on the project knows the epic map), rephrase to "a post-v1.0.0 cleanup pass" (external-reader-friendly), or drop the clause entirely?
- [ ] [Review][Patch] Quoted deprecation text in CONTRIBUTING truncates the actual script output [`CONTRIBUTING.md:84`] — prose says the stubs print `[DEPRECATED] Use .github/workflows/release.yaml via GitHub Actions.` (period, stop) but the actual `echo` message is `[DEPRECATED] Use .github/workflows/release.yaml via GitHub Actions. See docs/RELEASING.md.`. A reader grepping their terminal for the quoted string finds a prefix match, but the quote misrepresents the full message. Fix: extend the quote to include `See docs/RELEASING.md.` or replace with a shorter form like "prints a `[DEPRECATED]` warning".
- [ ] [Review][Patch] Double-negative phrasing "muscle memory doesn't accidentally ship anything" [`CONTRIBUTING.md:84`] — clearer as "so that muscle-memory `npm run release` invocations can't ship anything" or similar single-negative form.
- [x] [Review][Defer] Cross-file anchor `docs/RELEASING.md#rollback-playbook` not covered by CI link validator [`CONTRIBUTING.md:87`] — deferred, pre-existing tooling gap. `tools/validate-doc-links.js` scans only `docs/`, `tools/validate-file-refs.js` scans only `src/`; neither catches `CONTRIBUTING.md` anchor rot. If a future RELEASING.md refactor renames `## Rollback Playbook`, the CONTRIBUTING link silently 404s on GitHub with no CI signal. Out of scope for Story 4.2 (pre-existing validator coverage); captured in deferred-work.md for downstream tooling work.

**Dismissed (11) — noise or verified safe:**

- Cross-shell cmd.exe portability — `CONTRIBUTING.md:21` declares Linux/macOS only, Windows not supported.
- Double-print / dead tail on `release` alias — intentional per AC 5; tail preserved so Story 6.2 can remove via pure key-delete. Under POSIX `sh -c`, `exit 1` terminates before `;` is reached; verified sh/bash/dash.
- `version_bump` choices (`alpha`/`beta`/`rc`/`patch`/`minor`/`major`) — exact match to `release.yaml:18-23` workflow_dispatch inputs.
- "Seven-scenario" vs commit message "six-scenario" — `docs/RELEASING.md` has Scenarios A–G (7 scenarios) after the 12 code-review patches in `e597825`.
- Anchor `#rollback-playbook` existence — verified present on both `main` (as stub) and `feat/release-workflow` (full playbook).
- Intermediate merge-state readers of `main` see stub playbook — merge-sequence concern; Stories 4.1+4.2 ship via the same PR.
- Message lacks "maintainers only" framing in the `echo` stanza — 80-char budget tight; CONTRIBUTING.md:81 provides the framing.
- Em-dash density in the new section — subjective; matches SKF voice conventions elsewhere.
- Exit code `1` as deprecation sentinel — no project convention for distinguishing deprecation from failure.
- Story Dev Notes § Live-state recon claim that Story 4.1 merged via PR #194 — cosmetic inaccuracy in the story file itself (not in diff); AC 3 link target still resolves on `main` regardless. Flagged in Completion Notes, not in scope to fix here.
- SIGPIPE-on-echo edge case undermining `&&` short-circuit — extremely narrow; default SIGPIPE disposition kills the shell before `&&` is evaluated; spec AC 5 is verbatim on the `&& exit 1 ;` form.

content.content:
1	# Deferred Work
2	
3	Items pulled out of review/implementation to preserve scope, tracked here so they don't get lost.
4	
5	## Deferred from: code review of 1-1-audit-and-harden-main-branch-protection (2026-04-20)
6	
7	- **Restore snippet covers ruleset-edit recovery but not ruleset-deletion** — `docs/RELEASING.md:74-80` shows a `PUT` against an existing ruleset id. If the ruleset is deleted, the restore requires `POST /repos/{owner}/{repo}/rulesets` with the full ruleset body (including `name`, `target`, `enforcement`, `conditions`). Story 4.1 owns the full rollback playbook and is the natural home for this expansion.
8	
9	## Deferred from: code review of 1-2-create-release-github-environment-with-required-reviewer (2026-04-20)
10	
11	- **Hard-coded ruleset id `13855503` and environment id `14347249917` lack a re-derive path** — `docs/RELEASING.md:14,69`. If either resource is deleted and recreated the id changes and every hard-coded URL 404s. Swap id references for name-based lookups (`gh api repos/.../rulesets --jq '.[] | select(.name=="Default") | .id'`). Cross-cuts Story 4.1 rollback playbook.
12	- **"Zero env secrets" invariant has no CI assertion** — `docs/RELEASING.md:77,82`. Doc says "re-audit"; a pre-release CI check calling `/environments/release/secrets` and failing on `total_count > 0` would turn prose into an enforced gate. Belongs with the `release.yaml` workflow (Story 3.1).
13	- **Required-status-checks list drifts silently if `quality.yaml` job names or matrix values change** — `docs/RELEASING.md:26-34`. Doc warns itself ("update both in the same PR") but nothing enforces it; a pre-commit or CI step could diff the ruleset's check list against the workflow's job/matrix keys. Tooling improvement outside Epic 1 scope.
14	- **`prevent_self_review: false` → `true` flip on second-reviewer onboarding relies on prose memory** — `docs/RELEASING.md:80`. No reminder fires when a second reviewer is added. Low probability until solo-maintainer changes; revisit with team-growth.
15	- **`gh auth` scopes and `jq` dependency are unstated preconditions** — `docs/RELEASING.md:47-65,126-140`. A maintainer with a read-only token sees opaque 403s; `jq` absence on a fresh macOS machine breaks the restore block. Prepend a "Preconditions" subsection in a later polish pass.
16	- **Baseline.json capture procedure unspecified** — `docs/RELEASING.md:52-65`. Restore is aspirational if no baseline has been captured; needs a companion capture command and guidance on where baselines live. Story 4.1 rollback playbook is the natural home.
17	- **Site-nav discoverability of `docs/RELEASING.md` (Starlight/Astro sidebar) unverified** — `docs/RELEASING.md:1-4`. Frontmatter is Starlight-compatible but no sidebar entry was audited; if the docs site is built with Starlight, readers arriving at the site may not find the page without a direct URL. Confirm docs-site build config before acting.
18	
19	## Deferred from: code review of 1-3-register-npm-trusted-publisher-for-release-yaml-workflow (2026-04-20)
20	
21	- **Story 3.3 precondition — neutralize `publish.yaml` tag-push trigger atomically with `release.yaml` merge to main** — `.github/workflows/publish.yaml:10,37` has `id-token: write` and `npm publish --provenance` with `NODE_AUTH_TOKEN` wired. Until Story 6.1 deletes it, a stray `v*` tag push will fire `publish.yaml` alongside (or instead of) `release.yaml`, producing publishes attributed to the wrong workflow in the provenance record — an audit-trail inconsistency, not a hard failure (token auth bypasses the TP matcher). When Story 3.3 merges `release.yaml` to main, in the same PR remove `publish.yaml`'s `v*` tag trigger (leave `workflow_dispatch` only as an emergency hatch) so the release path is single-rooted. Resolved as defer in Story 1.3 code review D2 to avoid scope creep into a doc-only story.
22	- **Story 3.1 precondition — workflow filename MUST be `release.yaml` (not `.yml`)** — the npm Trusted Publisher registered 2026-04-20 binds on filename `release.yaml` exactly. A `.yml` typo in Story 3.1 produces an opaque 404 on Story 3.2's alpha cut with no recovery path short of deleting-and-re-adding the TP. Re-read `docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher` before authoring the workflow. Enforcement is prose-only by design (Story 1.3 code review D1, resolved as defer): a stub file on main was rejected as overkill for a low-probability risk that is triple-guarded (story Dev Notes, RELEASING.md rename-coupling note, Story 3.2 alpha cut as live validator).
23	- **Solo-maintainer self-review weakness inherited by TP section** — `docs/RELEASING.md § Release Environment`. New `## npm Trusted Publisher` section relies on the "required reviewer" gate, which the adjacent Release Environment section admits is self-approved (`prevent_self_review: false`). The TP section does not re-acknowledge this — an auditor reading only the new section will overestimate the control. Belongs with the Story 1.2 self-review gap (already captured there).
24	- **Rollback Playbook heading is empty / TOC lands on no content** — `docs/RELEASING.md § Rollback Playbook`. Bare `## Rollback Playbook` heading follows the `<!-- Rollback Playbook — added in Story 4.1 -->` placeholder. Readers hitting the TOC click into an empty section until Story 4.1 lands. Story 4.1's natural territory.
25	- **`NPM_TOKEN` rotation/deletion before Story 6.3 has no documented fallback** — `docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher`. Section names the token as "last-resort token-based publish" if OIDC breaks, but does not cover the inverse failure: token rotated/revoked during an OIDC-broken window leaves both paths down. Incident-response design belongs in Story 6.3's removal plan.
26	- **No CI/test asserts the TP registration's shape matches repo reality** — docs-only change; no guard. Drift between the npm-side TP and the `release.yaml`/`release` env it binds to is only caught at publish time (Story 3.2 onward). A CI job that reads the RELEASING.md table and verifies (a) `.github/workflows/release.yaml` exists, (b) environment `release` exists, (c) `package.json` name matches, would turn prose into an enforced gate. Standalone story once Story 3.x lands.
27	
28	## Deferred from: code review of 2-1-add-conventional-changelog-cli-as-a-devdependency (2026-04-20)
29	
30	- **CI-log deprecation-warning noise persists until Story 3.1 tooling decision** — `.github/workflows/*.yaml` via `npm ci`. Every CI run prints `npm warn deprecated conventional-changelog-cli@5.0.0: This package is no longer maintained.` Not a failure, but permanent log noise until the "Story 3.1 tooling decision" (below) resolves one way or the other. Acknowledged so future reviewers don't miss other deprecation notices amongst the repeated line.

# Deferred Work

Items pulled out of review/implementation to preserve scope, tracked here so they don't get lost.

## Deferred from: code review of 1-1-audit-and-harden-main-branch-protection (2026-04-20)

- **Restore snippet covers ruleset-edit recovery but not ruleset-deletion** — `docs/RELEASING.md:74-80` shows a `PUT` against an existing ruleset id. If the ruleset is deleted, the restore requires `POST /repos/{owner}/{repo}/rulesets` with the full ruleset body (including `name`, `target`, `enforcement`, `conditions`). Story 4.1 owns the full rollback playbook and is the natural home for this expansion.

## Deferred from: code review of 1-2-create-release-github-environment-with-required-reviewer (2026-04-20)

- **Hard-coded ruleset id `13855503` and environment id `14347249917` lack a re-derive path** — `docs/RELEASING.md:14,69`. If either resource is deleted and recreated the id changes and every hard-coded URL 404s. Swap id references for name-based lookups (`gh api repos/.../rulesets --jq '.[] | select(.name=="Default") | .id'`). Cross-cuts Story 4.1 rollback playbook.
- **"Zero env secrets" invariant has no CI assertion** — `docs/RELEASING.md:77,82`. Doc says "re-audit"; a pre-release CI check calling `/environments/release/secrets` and failing on `total_count > 0` would turn prose into an enforced gate. Belongs with the `release.yaml` workflow (Story 3.1).
- **Required-status-checks list drifts silently if `quality.yaml` job names or matrix values change** — `docs/RELEASING.md:26-34`. Doc warns itself ("update both in the same PR") but nothing enforces it; a pre-commit or CI step could diff the ruleset's check list against the workflow's job/matrix keys. Tooling improvement outside Epic 1 scope.
- **`prevent_self_review: false` → `true` flip on second-reviewer onboarding relies on prose memory** — `docs/RELEASING.md:80`. No reminder fires when a second reviewer is added. Low probability until solo-maintainer changes; revisit with team-growth.
- **`gh auth` scopes and `jq` dependency are unstated preconditions** — `docs/RELEASING.md:47-65,126-140`. A maintainer with a read-only token sees opaque 403s; `jq` absence on a fresh macOS machine breaks the restore block. Prepend a "Preconditions" subsection in a later polish pass.
- **Baseline.json capture procedure unspecified** — `docs/RELEASING.md:52-65`. Restore is aspirational if no baseline has been captured; needs a companion capture command and guidance on where baselines live. Story 4.1 rollback playbook is the natural home.
- **Site-nav discoverability of `docs/RELEASING.md` (Starlight/Astro sidebar) unverified** — `docs/RELEASING.md:1-4`. Frontmatter is Starlight-compatible but no sidebar entry was audited; if the docs site is built with Starlight, readers arriving at the site may not find the page without a direct URL. Confirm docs-site build config before acting.

When Story 3.3 merges `release.yaml` to main, in the same PR remove `publish.yaml`'s `v*` tag trigger (leave `workflow_dispatch` only as an emergency hatch) so the release path is single-rooted. Resolved as defer in Story 1.3 code review D2 to avoid scope creep into a doc-only story.

A `.yml` typo in Story 3.1 produces an opaque 404 on Story 3.2's alpha cut with no recovery path short of deleting-and-re-adding the TP. Re-read `docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher` before authoring the workflow. content.content:
95	- **Scenario G detection misses ghost-registered workflows after file deletion** — `docs/RELEASING.md:+240-244` (Story 4.1). GitHub keeps a workflow `state:"active"` for some time after its file is deleted from the default branch. Edge-Hunter live-verified this on the SKF repo: `env-gate-test.yaml` returns `state:"active"` despite the file being absent. If `release.yaml` is deleted via PR, the same lag applies — the AC 9.b detection passes, the next dispatch fails with `Workflow does not exist`, and the responder concludes the playbook check is reliable when it isn't. Augment with a file-presence check on `main` (e.g., `gh api repos/.../contents/.github/workflows/release.yaml`) or `git ls-tree main -- .github/workflows/release.yaml`. Bundle with any future Scenario G hardening pass.
96	- **Scenario G `gh workflow enable` requires admin permissions** — `docs/RELEASING.md:+252` (Story 4.1). A responder with `triage` or `maintain` role cannot enable workflows; only `admin` can. SKF is single-maintainer today so this gap is dormant. When a second maintainer onboards (Epic 1 Carry-forward #3 — `prevent_self_review: true`), document the ACL requirement and an escalation path or default to the emergency hatch.
97	- **Scenario A → B boundary blurred by npm CDN cache lag** — `docs/RELEASING.md:+36, +59` (Story 4.1). `npm view <pkg> dist-tags` reads through a CDN with multi-second to multi-minute eventual consistency. "Still shows `<bad>` on `latest`" is not a reliable "no users yet" signal — installs hit different cache nodes. A maintainer running `npm view` 60s after publish can see `<bad>` on `latest` while CI bots in another region have already pulled from a warmer node and committed lockfiles pinning `<bad>`. Add a freshness cross-check (`npm view ... --json | jq '.time | to_entries | last'`) or an explicit "this is a quick visual check, not authoritative" caveat.
98	- **Scenario C 72h boundary uses npm-clock, not local-clock** — `docs/RELEASING.md:+89` (Story 4.1). The "less than 72 hours since publish" check should be computed from `npm view <pkg>@<bad> time`, not the responder's local `date`. At the boundary, npm's policy engine uses its server timestamp; a guess off by a few minutes 422s the unpublish. Add a one-line "compute from `npm view ... time`" caveat.
99	- **Scenario C "zero downloads" has 24-48h npm stats lag** — `docs/RELEASING.md:+89` (Story 4.1). The npm downloads API is documented as eventually consistent with up to 24-48h lag; a published version can show "0 downloads" while having been installed by hundreds of CI runs. Treating `npm view` and the implied download count as authoritative in the first 24-48h post-publish is unsafe. Add explicit warning: "the 'zero downloads' criterion is unreliable in the first 24-48h due to npm's stats lag. When in doubt, treat C as Scenario B."
100	- **Scenario D tag-deletion may be blocked by future tag-protection ruleset** — `docs/RELEASING.md:+121` (Story 4.1). The Default ruleset's `deletion` rule applies to branches; tag protection is a separate ruleset / rule type. The playbook does not state whether tag deletion on `origin` is currently allowed or blocked. If tag-deletion protection is added in any future PR (or if a `tag` rule is added to the existing ruleset), Scenario D's `git push --delete origin v<version>` silently 422s and the recovery dead-ends. Speculative — no rule today; revisit if a `tag` rule is added.
101	- **Scenario F UI revoke flow assumes 2FA intact** — `docs/RELEASING.md:+188-190` (Story 4.1). The "Visit npmjs.com → Settings → Trusted Publisher → Delete" path is gated on the maintainer's 2FA. The trigger list explicitly includes "npm support notifies of suspected compromise" — a strong signal that account access may itself be compromised. The first-step revoke fails closed in that case; only the later `support@npmjs.com` coordination path is documented as a fallback. Add an "Immediate CLI — account-locked variant" that routes through `support@npmjs.com` *before* attempting the UI flow if 2FA is degraded; reference npm's account-recovery URL.
102

content.text:
**Code review complete.** 2 `decision-needed`, 2 `patch`, 1 `defer`, 11 dismissed as noise.

Findings written to the Review Findings section of the story file. Defer entry also appended to `deferred-work.md`.

**Acceptance Auditor summary:** all 15 ACs PASS. AC 6 / 11 / 12 are runtime-only (fail-loud smoke test, quality gate, hooks) and trust the Dev Agent Record transcripts. No FAILs.

---

### Decision-needed findings — need your call before patches

**Decision 1 — Deprecation message on stdout vs stderr** (`package.json:48-52`)

- Current: `echo '[DEPRECATED] ...' && exit 1 ; tail`
- Concern: By convention, diagnostic/deprecation warnings go to stderr. A user piping `npm run release:patch | tee build.log` (or CI log scrapers that split streams) sees the warning mingled with stdout.
- Counter: AC 5 pins the prefix string verbatim — adding `>&2` diverges from spec literal. The message still reaches an interactive terminal unchanged.

Options:
1. **Patch** — change to `echo '...' >&2 && exit 1 ; tail` on all five keys (divergence from AC 5 verbatim).
2. **Dismiss** — keep spec-verbatim; interactive invocation is the dominant case.

- **If `docs:validate-drift` fails,** you either touched a pinned version/commit SHA that no longer resolves in [oh-my-skills](https://github.com/armelhbobdad/oh-my-skills), or you added a library reference the whitelist doesn't cover. CI re-runs everything on the PR. A green local run and a red CI run means either (a) you have uncommitted files, or (b) your Node/uv versions drift from `.nvmrc` / `test:python`. Check both before filing a CI bug.

## Releasing

Maintainers only — if you're not cutting a release, skip this section.

- **Canonical path:** `.github/workflows/release.yaml`, triggered via GitHub Actions → Run workflow → choose `version_bump` (`alpha` / `beta` / `rc` / `patch` / `minor` / `major`). That is the only supported route — OIDC-backed publish, required-reviewer gate on the `release` environment, auto-provenance on the npm tarball.
- **Do not use `npm run release:*` locally.** Every `release:*` script (plus the bare `release` alias) is retained only as a fail-loud stub that prints `[DEPRECATED] Use .github/workflows/release.yaml via GitHub Actions.` and exits 1. The stubs exist so that muscle memory doesn't accidentally ship anything; Epic 6 removes them outright post-v1.0.0.
- **Do not invoke `publish.yaml` or `manual-release.yaml` directly.** Both are DEPRECATED (see each workflow's top-of-file comment header) and scheduled for deletion post-v1.0.0 per Epic 6.

See [docs/RELEASING.md](docs/RELEASING.md) for the full procedure — branch-protection rules, the `release` environment with its required-reviewer gate, npm Trusted Publisher registration, and the seven-scenario [rollback playbook](docs/RELEASING.md#rollback-playbook).

## Adding a New Workflow Skill

The `src/skf-*/` directories each follow the same shape:

```
src/skf-<name>/

attachment.snippet:
1	# Contributing to Skill Forge
2	
3	SKF turns code and docs into verified agent skills. Every instruction traces back to a real line of source. See [README.md](README.md) for the pitch; this file covers how to land changes without setting the test suite on fire.
4	
5	SKF is a [BMAD](https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD) module. For BMAD philosophy, framework conventions, and module-authoring patterns in general, start at [docs.bmad-method.org](https://docs.bmad-method.org). This doc stays scoped to what's SKF-specific.
6	
7	## What You Can Contribute
8	
9	- **Workflows** (`src/skf-*/`) — new skill-compilation or lifecycle flows. Example: a `skf-diff-skill` that compares two versions of the same skill and emits a migration note.
10	- **Knowledge fragments** (`src/knowledge/`) — cross-workflow principles Ferris loads just-in-time. Example: a new `security-review.md` that captures rules reused by CS, QS, and AS.
11	- **Forger assets** (`src/forger/`, `src/shared/`) — shared agent memory, preferences, or helpers (e.g. tier detection, health-check templates).
12	- **Validators** (`tools/validate-*.js`) — deterministic checks that run in `npm run quality`. Example: a new validator that flags `{installed_path}` leaks in step files.
13	- **Docs** (`docs/`, `website/`) — tutorial / reference / explanation content surfaced at [armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge](https://armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/).
14	- **Ecosystem integrations** — new tool bridges (ast-grep, cocoindex, QMD, tessl, Snyk, graphify-style indexers) wired through the tier-aware discovery path.
15	- **Bug reports** — always useful, especially if they come in via the workflow health-check loop (see below).
16	
17	If you're not sure where a change belongs, open an issue and ask before writing code.
18	
19	## Local Setup
20	
21	**Platforms:** Linux and macOS. Windows is not supported (POSIX-only shell tooling, symlinks, path quoting). WSL2 works.
22	
23	**Prerequisites:**
24	
25	- [Node.js](https://nodejs.org/) >= 22 (see `.nvmrc`)
26	- [Python](https://www.python.org/) >= 3.10
27	- [uv](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/) — runs the Python test suite
28	- `git`, `gh` — used by several workflows and by the health-check loop
29	
30	```bash
31	git clone https://github.com/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge.git
32	cd bmad-module-skill-forge
33	npm install           # also wires husky pre-commit hooks via "prepare"
34	npm run quality       # run the full local pre-flight
35	```
36	
37	The `npm run quality` script is your contract with CI. It runs:
38	
39	- `format:check` (Prettier), `lint` (ESLint), `lint:md` (markdownlint)
40	- `test:schemas`, `test:install`, `test:cli`, `test:workflow`, `test:python`, `test:knowledge`
41	- `validate:schemas`, `validate:skills`, `validate:refs`
42	- `docs:validate-drift` — SKF docs vs. the canonical [oh-my-skills](https://github.com/armelhbobdad/oh-my-skills) output
43	
44	If `npm run quality` passes locally, CI should too. The same steps run in [`.github/workflows/quality.yaml`](.github/workflows/quality.yaml) on every pull request.
45	
46	## Workflow for Changes
47	
48	1. **Branch from `main`.** Name it like the commit scope: `fix/skf-test-skill-...`, `feat/health-check-...`, `docs/...`.
49	2. **Match the commit-message convention from the git log.** SKF uses conventional-commit prefixes with a scoped subsystem:
50	   - `feat(skf-create-skill): ...`
51	   - `fix(health-check): ...`
52	   - `docs(readme): ...`
53	   - `ci(health-check): ...`
54	   - `refactor(skf-create-skill): ...`
55	   - `chore: ...` (no scope needed)
56	
57	   `git log --oneline -20` is the authoritative style guide. Match what you see.
58	
59	3. **Reference issues with `Fixes #NNN`** in the PR body (and optionally in the commit trailer). Use **same-repo GitHub issue numbers only** — do not reference internal IDs under `_bmad-output/todo/` or elsewhere; those are author notes, not public contracts.
60	4. **Pre-commit hooks run automatically** via husky + lint-staged: `eslint --fix`, `prettier --write`, and `markdownlint-cli2` on `.md` files. They run on staged files only.
61	5. **PR description:** explain _why_. What was broken, what does this change, and how did you verify it? Keep it honest and short. The template in [.github/](.github/) is a starting point; ignore the sections that don't apply.
62	6. **If you used Claude (or any AI assistant)** to help write a non-trivial chunk of the change, add a `Co-Authored-By:` trailer to the commit — SKF's recent history uses the format:
63	
64	   ```
65	   Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
66	   ```
67	
68	   Not mandatory, but we prefer accurate attribution over silent ghostwriting.
69	
70	## The Quality Gate
71	
72	`npm run quality` must pass before you push. If it fails:
73	
74	- **Fix the root cause.** Do not `git commit --no-verify`. Do not disable a rule to make the linter shut up. If a hook is wrong, fix the hook in a separate PR.
75	- **If a Python test fails on your machine but not in CI,** check your `uv` version and re-run `npm run test:python` from a clean shell.
76	- **If `docs:validate-drift` fails,** you either touched a pinned version/commit SHA that no longer resolves in [oh-my-skills](https://github.com/armelhbobdad/oh-my-skills), or you added a library reference the whitelist doesn't cover. Fix the reference; don't relax the validator unless the fix is clearly out of scope.
77	
78	CI re-runs everything on the PR. A green local run and a red CI run means either (a) you have uncommitted files, or (b) your Node/uv versions drift from `.nvmrc` / `test:python`. Check both before filing a CI bug.
79	
80	## Releasing
81	
82	Maintainers only — if you're not cutting a release, skip this section.
83	
84	- **Canonical path:** `.github/workflows/release.yaml`, triggered via GitHub Actions → Run workflow → choose `version_bump` (`alpha` / `beta` / `rc` / `patch` / `minor` / `major`). That is the only supported route — OIDC-backed publish, required-reviewer gate on the `release` environment, auto-provenance on the npm tarball.
85	- **Do not use `npm run release:*` locally.** Every `release:*` script (plus the bare `release` alias) is retained only as a fail-loud stub: it prints a `[DEPRECATED]` warning pointing at the canonical path and exits 1. The stubs exist to catch muscle-memory `npm run release` invocations before they ship anything; a post-v1.0.0 cleanup pass removes them outright.
86	- **Do not invoke `publish.yaml` or `manual-release.yaml` directly.** Both are DEPRECATED (see each workflow's top-of-file comment header) and scheduled for deletion as part of that same post-v1.0.0 cleanup.
87	
88	See [docs/RELEASING.md](docs/RELEASING.md) for the full procedure — branch-protection rules, the `release` environment with its required-reviewer gate, npm Trusted Publisher registration, and the seven-scenario [rollback playbook](docs/RELEASING.md#rollback-playbook).
89	
90	## Adding a New Workflow Skill
91	
92	The `src/skf-*/` directories each follow the same shape:
93	
94	```
95	src/skf-<name>/
96	  SKILL.md            # frontmatter (name, description, "Use when ..."), stages table
97	  steps-c/            # one file per step, loaded one-at-a-time by Ferris
98	  references/         # step-scoped rules, protocols, decision tables
99	  assets/             # step-scoped templates, schemas, output formats
100	```
101	
102	- **Start from an existing skill** with similar shape — `skf-quick-skill` is the simplest, `skf-create-skill` is the reference for the full pipeline.
103	- **Or scaffold with BMAD tooling** — the `bmad-workflow-builder` skill builds / edits / converts workflows interactively; `@Ferris CS` (skf-create-skill) is the content-extraction pattern SKF uses for its own skills in the wild.
104	- **Frontmatter matters.** `validate:skills` enforces SKILL-01 through STEP-07 (see [`tools/validate-skills.js`](tools/validate-skills.js)): SKILL.md must have `name` + `description` with a "Use when" / "Use if" trigger; step files must not have `name`/`description`; step count must be 2–10; step filenames must match `step-NN-<slug>.md`.

cp package.json /tmp/pkg.backup.json && git tag > /tmp/tags.before.txt
for s in release release:major release:minor release:patch release:prerelease; do
  OUT=$(npm run "$s" 2>&1 1>/dev/null); RC=$?
  STDOUT_OUT=$(npm run "$s" 2>/dev/null); RC2=$?
  echo "[$s] rc=$RC"
  echo "$OUT" | grep -q '\[DEPRECATED\]' && echo "  PASS: stderr contains [DEPRECATED]" || echo "  FAIL: stderr missing [DEPRECATED]"
  echo "$STDOUT_OUT" | grep -q '\[DEPRECATED\]' && echo "  NOTE: stdout also contains [DEPRECATED] (expected: no)" || echo "  PASS: stdout does NOT contain [DEPRECATED]"
  [ "$RC" -ne 0 ] && echo "  PASS: non-zero exit code" || echo "  FAIL: exit code 0"
done
git tag > /tmp/tags.after.txt
diff -q /tmp/tags.before.txt /tmp/tags.after.txt && echo "PASS: no new tags"
diff -q /tmp/pkg.backup.json package.json && echo "PASS: package.json version unchanged"

file.content:
# Story 4.2: Mark legacy release paths as deprecated in `CONTRIBUTING.md` and legacy workflow files

Status: review

<!-- Note: Validation is optional. Run validate-create-story for quality check before dev-story. -->

content.content:
430	| 2026-04-23 | 4.2   | Added `## Releasing` section to CONTRIBUTING.md naming the canonical `release.yaml` path; prefixed all five `release*` scripts in `package.json` with a fail-loud `[DEPRECATED]` stanza. Commit `84affc3`. | Amelia |
431	
432	### Review Findings
433	
434	_Code review against commit `84affc3` on 2026-04-23 — 3 layers: Blind Hunter (no context), Edge Case Hunter (repo read), Acceptance Auditor (spec + 15 ACs). 15 findings raised, 11 dismissed. Acceptance Auditor verdict: all 15 ACs PASS (AC 6/11/12 are runtime-only and trust the Dev Agent Record smoke-test transcript)._
435	
436	- [ ] [Review][Decision] Deprecation message on stdout instead of stderr — `package.json:48-52` writes `echo '[DEPRECATED] ...' && exit 1 ; tail`. Convention is that deprecation/diagnostic warnings go to stderr (`echo '...' >&2`). Current behavior: the message still reaches an interactive terminal but a user piping `npm run release:patch | tee build.log` or similar will see the warning mingled with normal stdout output, and automation that separates stdout from stderr won't classify it as a warning. Decision: AC 5 fixes the prefix string verbatim — adding `>&2` diverges from the spec literal. Accept spec as-is (dismiss) or diverge for convention (patch)?
437	- [ ] [Review][Decision] Internal "Epic 6" jargon leaks into outward-facing CONTRIBUTING.md — `CONTRIBUTING.md:84-85` says "Epic 6 removes them outright post-v1.0.0" and "scheduled for deletion post-v1.0.0 per Epic 6". External contributors reading CONTRIBUTING.md have no way to resolve "Epic 6" — it's a `_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/epics.md` reference invisible from outside the repo's internal planning tree. Decision: keep the Epic reference (internal-consistency signal — everyone on the project knows the epic map), rephrase to "a post-v1.0.0 cleanup pass" (external-reader-friendly), or drop the clause entirely?
438	- [ ] [Review][Patch] Quoted deprecation text in CONTRIBUTING truncates the actual script output [`CONTRIBUTING.md:84`] — prose says the stubs print `[DEPRECATED] Use .github/workflows/release.yaml via GitHub Actions.` (period, stop) but the actual `echo` message is `[DEPRECATED] Use .github/workflows/release.yaml via GitHub Actions. See docs/RELEASING.md.`. A reader grepping their terminal for the quoted string finds a prefix match, but the quote misrepresents the full message. Fix: extend the quote to include `See docs/RELEASING.md.` or replace with a shorter form like "prints a `[DEPRECATED]` warning".
439	- [ ] [Review][Patch] Double-negative phrasing "muscle memory doesn't accidentally ship anything" [`CONTRIBUTING.md:84`] — clearer as "so that muscle-memory `npm run release` invocations can't ship anything" or similar single-negative form.
440	- [x] [Review][Defer] Cross-file anchor `docs/RELEASING.md#rollback-playbook` not covered by CI link validator [`CONTRIBUTING.md:87`] — deferred, pre-existing tooling gap. `tools/validate-doc-links.js` scans only `docs/`, `tools/validate-file-refs.js` scans only `src/`; neither catches `CONTRIBUTING.md` anchor rot. If a future RELEASING.md refactor renames `## Rollback Playbook`, the CONTRIBUTING link silently 404s on GitHub with no CI signal. Out of scope for Story 4.2 (pre-existing validator coverage); captured in deferred-work.md for downstream tooling work.
441	
442	**Dismissed (11) — noise or verified safe:**
443	
444	- Cross-shell cmd.exe portability — `CONTRIBUTING.md:21` declares Linux/macOS only, Windows not supported.
445	- Double-print / dead tail on `release` alias — intentional per AC 5; tail preserved so Story 6.2 can remove via pure key-delete. Under POSIX `sh -c`, `exit 1` terminates before `;` is reached; verified sh/bash/dash.
446	- `version_bump` choices (`alpha`/`beta`/`rc`/`patch`/`minor`/`major`) — exact match to `release.yaml:18-23` workflow_dispatch inputs.
447	- "Seven-scenario" vs commit message "six-scenario" — `docs/RELEASING.md` has Scenarios A–G (7 scenarios) after the 12 code-review patches in `e597825`.
448	- Anchor `#rollback-playbook` existence — verified present on both `main` (as stub) and `feat/release-workflow` (full playbook).
449	- Intermediate merge-state readers of `main` see stub playbook — merge-sequence concern; Stories 4.1+4.2 ship via the same PR.
450	- Message lacks "maintainers only" framing in the `echo` stanza — 80-char budget tight; CONTRIBUTING.md:81 provides the framing.
451	- Em-dash density in the new section — subjective; matches SKF voice conventions elsewhere.
452	- Exit code `1` as deprecation sentinel — no project convention for distinguishing deprecation from failure.
453	- Story Dev Notes § Live-state recon claim that Story 4.1 merged via PR #194 — cosmetic inaccuracy in the story file itself (not in diff); AC 3 link target still resolves on `main` regardless. Flagged in Completion Notes, not in scope to fix here.
454	- SIGPIPE-on-echo edge case undermining `&&` short-circuit — extremely narrow; default SIGPIPE disposition kills the shell before `&&` is evaluated; spec AC 5 is verbatim on the `&& exit 1 ;` form.

| 2026-04-23 | 4.2   | Added `## Releasing` section to CONTRIBUTING.md naming the canonical `release.yaml` path; prefixed all five `release*` scripts in `package.json` with a fail-loud `[DEPRECATED]` stanza. Commit `84affc3`. | Amelia |

### Review Findings

_Code review against commit `84affc3` on 2026-04-23 — 3 layers: Blind Hunter (no context), Edge Case Hunter (repo read), Acceptance Auditor (spec + 15 ACs). 15 findings raised, 11 dismissed. Acceptance Auditor verdict: all 15 ACs PASS (AC 6/11/12 are runtime-only and trust the Dev Agent Record smoke-test transcript)._

- [ ] [Review][Decision] Deprecation message on stdout instead of stderr — `package.json:48-52` writes `echo '[DEPRECATED] ...' && exit 1 ; tail`. Convention is that deprecation/diagnostic warnings go to stderr (`echo '...' >&2`). Current behavior: the message still reaches an interactive terminal but a user piping `npm run release:patch | tee build.log` or similar will see the warning mingled with normal stdout output, and automation that separates stdout from stderr won't classify it as a warning. Decision: AC 5 fixes the prefix string verbatim — adding `>&2` diverges from the spec literal. Accept spec as-is (dismiss) or diverge for convention (patch)?
- [ ] [Review][Decision] Internal "Epic 6" jargon leaks into outward-facing CONTRIBUTING.md — `CONTRIBUTING.md:84-85` says "Epic 6 removes them outright post-v1.0.0" and "scheduled for deletion post-v1.0.0 per Epic 6". External contributors reading CONTRIBUTING.md have no way to resolve "Epic 6" — it's a `_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/epics.md` reference invisible from outside the repo's internal planning tree. Decision: keep the Epic reference (internal-consistency signal — everyone on the project knows the epic map), rephrase to "a post-v1.0.0 cleanup pass" (external-reader-friendly), or drop the clause entirely?
- [ ] [Review][Patch] Quoted deprecation text in CONTRIBUTING truncates the actual script output [`CONTRIBUTING.md:84`] — prose says the stubs print `[DEPRECATED] Use .github/workflows/release.yaml via GitHub Actions.` (period, stop) but the actual `echo` message is `[DEPRECATED] Use .github/workflows/release.yaml via GitHub Actions. See docs/RELEASING.md.`. A reader grepping their terminal for the quoted string finds a prefix match, but the quote misrepresents the full message. Fix: extend the quote to include `See docs/RELEASING.md.` or replace with a shorter form like "prints a `[DEPRECATED]` warning".
- [ ] [Review][Patch] Double-negative phrasing "muscle memory doesn't accidentally ship anything" [`CONTRIBUTING.md:84`] — clearer as "so that muscle-memory `npm run release` invocations can't ship anything" or similar single-negative form.
- [x] [Review][Defer] Cross-file anchor `docs/RELEASING.md#rollback-playbook` not covered by CI link validator [`CONTRIBUTING.md:87`] — deferred, pre-existing tooling gap. `tools/validate-doc-links.js` scans only `docs/`, `tools/validate-file-refs.js` scans only `src/`; neither catches `CONTRIBUTING.md` anchor rot. If a future RELEASING.md refactor renames `## Rollback Playbook`, the CONTRIBUTING link silently 404s on GitHub with no CI signal. Out of scope for Story 4.2 (pre-existing validator coverage); captured in deferred-work.md for downstream tooling work.

**Dismissed (11) — noise or verified safe:**

- Cross-shell cmd.exe portability — `CONTRIBUTING.md:21` declares Linux/macOS only, Windows not supported.
- Double-print / dead tail on `release` alias — intentional per AC 5; tail preserved so Story 6.2 can remove via pure key-delete. Under POSIX `sh -c`, `exit 1` terminates before `;` is reached; verified sh/bash/dash.
- `version_bump` choices (`alpha`/`beta`/`rc`/`patch`/`minor`/`major`) — exact match to `release.yaml:18-23` workflow_dispatch inputs.
- "Seven-scenario" vs commit message "six-scenario" — `docs/RELEASING.md` has Scenarios A–G (7 scenarios) after the 12 code-review patches in `e597825`.
- Anchor `#rollback-playbook` existence — verified present on both `main` (as stub) and `feat/release-workflow` (full playbook).
- Intermediate merge-state readers of `main` see stub playbook — merge-sequence concern; Stories 4.1+4.2 ship via the same PR.
- Message lacks "maintainers only" framing in the `echo` stanza — 80-char budget tight; CONTRIBUTING.md:81 provides the framing.
- Em-dash density in the new section — subjective; matches SKF voice conventions elsewhere.
- Exit code `1` as deprecation sentinel — no project convention for distinguishing deprecation from failure.
- Story Dev Notes § Live-state recon claim that Story 4.1 merged via PR #194 — cosmetic inaccuracy in the story file itself (not in diff); AC 3 link target still resolves on `main` regardless. Flagged in Completion Notes, not in scope to fix here.
- SIGPIPE-on-echo edge case undermining `&&` short-circuit — extremely narrow; default SIGPIPE disposition kills the shell before `&&` is evaluated; spec AC 5 is verbatim on the `&& exit 1 ;` form.

Added `>&2` to the echo on all five `release:*` keys so the `[DEPRECATED]` warning goes to stderr (convention for diagnostic/deprecation notices). This diverges from AC 5's verbatim prefix string; flagged here so Story 5.1 readiness audit and Story 6.2 delete-script know the divergence is intentional. Verified via re-run smoke loop: all five `rc=1`, `[DEPRECATED]` on stderr, no new tags, `version` unchanged. (npm's own `> <cmd>` command-echo still lands on stdout — that is npm's behavior, not our script's, and is suppressible via `--silent`.)

Both bullets rephrased: the `release:*` stubs bullet now ends "a post-v1.0.0 cleanup pass removes them outright"; the legacy-workflows bullet ends "scheduled for deletion as part of that same post-v1.0.0 cleanup". External contributors reading CONTRIBUTING.md get the when/why without needing to resolve the internal epic-map reference. Story 6.1 / 6.2 / 6.3 are the actual cleanup-pass implementations; their internal epic-ID framing is unchanged in planning docs.
- [x] [Review][Patch] Truncated deprecation quote replaced with short form — `CONTRIBUTING.md:85`. Prose now says the stubs print "a `[DEPRECATED]` warning pointing at the canonical path and exits 1" (no partial quote of the actual echo message). Eliminates the quote-vs-reality mismatch entirely.
- [x] [Review][Patch] Double-negative tightened — `CONTRIBUTING.md:85`. Replaced "so that muscle memory doesn't accidentally ship anything" with "to catch muscle-memory `npm run release` invocations before they ship anything" (positive framing).

# Epic Status:
#   - backlog: Epic not yet started
#   - in-progress: Epic actively being worked on
#   - done: All stories in epic completed
#
# Epic Status Transitions:
#   - backlog → in-progress: Automatically when first story is created (via create-story)
#   - in-progress → done: Manually when all stories reach 'done' status
#
# Story Status:
#   - backlog: Story only exists in epic file
#   - ready-for-dev: Story file created in stories folder
#   - in-progress: Developer actively working on implementation
#   - review: Ready for code review (via Dev's code-review workflow)
#   - done: Story completed
#
# Retrospective Status:
#   - optional: Can be completed but not required
#   - done: Retrospective has been completed
#
# WORKFLOW NOTES:

# - Epic transitions to 'in-progress' automatically when first story is created
# - Stories can be worked in parallel if team capacity allows
# - Developer typically creates next story after previous one is 'done' to incorporate learnings
# - Dev moves story to 'review', then runs code-review (fresh context, different LLM recommended)
